chunk_summary: This section outlines telemedicine compliance, consent, and documentation requirements for tele-nutrition in India, emphasizing RMP accountability, consent protocols, and mandatory record retention for at least three years.
learning_objectives: Understand the professional accountability of RMPs in tele-nutrition practices in India.
Learn the consent requirements for tele-nutrition consultations, especially for minors.
Identify the documentation and record retention guidelines for tele-nutrition services.
Recognize the limitations of telemedicine in emergency and pediatric nutrition scenarios.
key_facts: Tele-nutrition in India requires the same professional accountability as in-person care.
Consent is implied if the caregiver initiates the consult but must be explicit if initiated by the clinic.
For minors (<18 years), consent must come from a parent, adult sibling, or legal guardian.
Case records and digital trails must be retained for at least three years from the last consult.
Telemedicine is not recommended for emergencies unless limited to first aid, life-saving measures, counseling, or referral advice.
Prescriptions must be issued by RMPs, and therapeutic supplements or drugs cannot be prescribed by non-RMP staff.
reference_quote: Telemedicine guidance frames the clinical act as being delivered by a registered medical practitioner (RMP) and warns that remote assessment has intrinsic limits.
